patient_utterances: [Clinician asked: "hello  can you hear me ok?"] Hello. Hi.

[Clinician asked: "Nineteen years old OK Amanda, and how can I help you this  afternoon?"] Um, so it's being going on for a few days now. Um, it's not happened before and I'm bit worried. I've uh it quite hurts when I kind of pee.

[Clinician asked: "<INAUDIBLE_SPEECH/>"] Um, and yeah it's like I'm going to the toilet a lot more, um. And it's really, like I can't, like go to uni properly. I'm just like constantly looking for the toilet.
[Clinician asked: "<INAUDIBLE_SPEECH/>"] Um, and it kind of hurts sometimes when I'm, like in at the bottom of my tummy as well.

[Clinician asked: "Right OK. I'm sorry to hear there's a lot going on there and I can, see why it's affecting you. Um, so you mentioned it's been going on for last three days, mainly the burning pain when you pass urine. Um, you said you're going to the toilet more often as well, that right?"] Uh, yeah it's been going on for a little bit longer than three days I think actually, maybe just under a week. Um.
[Clinician asked: "Ohh and a week, sorry my . OK, uh."] Sorry no I  didn't explain it properly before, um yeah no I it's been going on for about just under a week.
[Clinician asked: "<INAUDIBLE_SPEECH/>"] Um, and yeah I  notice a sort of burning, stinging, when I when I go to the toilet, yeah.

[Clinician asked: "Are you noticing any, anything in your urine, predominantly blood or um, discolouration of urine?"] Um, I think, well today I noticed, um, that it was like a bit pink. The, the urine.

[Clinician asked: "<INAUDIBLE_SPEECH/> OK, uh <INAUDIBLE_SPEECH/> of any kind of frank blood? I mean red blood at all, in your urine? <INAUDIBLE_SPEECH/>"] Yeah, I think just today . Um, I think it was  probably but I don't know it was pink so.

[Clinician asked: "OK. Um, fine we'll we'll have to definitely explore that  in a little bit or  detail later on. Um, you also mentioned some tummy pain as well, can you tell me  a  bit more about that pain?"] Yeah it's kind of at the bottom of my tummy, um, uh mainly when I go to the loo, I think that's when it's worse.

[Clinician asked: "OK. Um, can you describe it  crampy pain? Is it more of a burning pain?"] Um, I wouldn't describe it as crampy I don't think it's like a period pain, it's a bit different, it's, it's hard to say, an achey pain I suppose.
[Clinician asked: "OK. An aching pain, OK. And does the pain move anywhere else? Does it move towards your back? Towards your sides?"] No, no, no.
[Clinician asked: "OK, all right. Um, any other symptoms? <INAUDIBLE_SPEECH/> Nausea? Vomiting?"] No, no nausea vomiting.
[Clinician asked: "OK. Any temperatures or fevers?"] Um no, no not that I've noticed.

[Clinician asked: "OK, all right. And how are your bowels at the moment? Are your bowels working OK?"] Uh I, I usually have a bit of problems with my bowels anyway, so I don't think it's any, different, from what it usually is.

[Clinician asked: "OK, and  when, when when you say you have problems, what kind of problems do you normally have?"] Um, I think I have, um, IBS, um, so I just kinda pass, uh, my bowels like quite, quite a lot.

[Clinician asked: "Right."] Um, and sometimes it's quite, it's quite variable like sometimes it's a bit softer, sometimes it's a bit,  sorry if it's a bit disgusting but like, sometimes it's a bit uh harder and kind of hard to predict.
[Clinician asked: "<INAUDIBLE_SPEECH/> OK, all right but, and you've noticed no new symptoms? No, no, no new symptoms in your bowels?"] Like, yeah OK.

[Clinician asked: "Uh. OK, all right. Um, and Amanda sometimes we have to ask quite sensitive questions as you can imagine. Um, uh, can I ask? Are you currently uh, are you currently sexually active? At the moment?"] No, no, not <INAUDIBLE_SPEECH/> Uh  uh yeah um, I have a, a long-term, um, boyfriend.
[Clinician asked: "OK, and um, have you noticed any other discharge from down below? So, from from the front passage?"] Hmm  no I don't think so. Nothing unusual.
[Clinician asked: "OK. Have you, do you use any form of contraception?"] Uh yeah, uh I'm um on the pill.
[Clinician asked: "The pill. What kind of pill is it? Is it combined pill or the mini-pill?"] Uh, combined pill.
[Clinician asked: "OK, fine. Um, when was the last time you had um, unprotected sex? If you don't want me asking."] Um, uh, I mean, mean unprotected as in like no, no condoms or anything?

[Clinician asked: "Yeah, exactly."] Um, I, I guess 'cause,  um, I don't really use condoms with my boyfriend,  like. I dunno we've just kind of agreed, uh, so um I guess, a week ago, two weeks ago , quite quite  .

[Clinician asked: "Yep, a week ago, OK all right. What I'm trying get at is whether um, uh there's any chance you could be pregnant at all. Uh, I know you're on the pill, but um we're we, nonetheless we do have to ask. Um when was your last, when was your last period?"] <INAUDIBLE_SPEECH/> <INAUDIBLE_SPEECH/> Um, two weeks ago.

[Clinician asked: "Two weeks ago OK, all right. Just something for us, to bear in mind, if your symptoms don't get any better OK, um. Fine, in terms of your general health otherwise at the moment are you, uh eating and drinking OK?"] Uh, yeah, yeah eating drinking OK. Um, I did have one kinda concern I dunno <INAUDIBLE_SPEECH/>

[Clinician asked: "<INAUDIBLE_SPEECH/>"] So I I smoked, uh this is quite bad, but I smoked weed for the first time. Uh, like a week and a half ago, I  I just wondered whether it's related at all, probably not but I just thought I'd get some advice from you.
[Clinician asked: "Sure, well thanks for sharing with me. Um, so, I, I don't think  it's  is related to your symptoms, I think it probably just pure pure coincidence, um."] Sure OK. Sure.
[Clinician asked: "But, uh, you know I do strongly advise you to you know, um, make it a regular habit if you can. Um, just for your, just for your general health moving forwards, OK?"] OK.

[Clinician asked: "Um, fine. Moving on, in terms of your health otherwise, do you have any other medical conditions  , apart from the IBS, is there anything else I should be aware of?"] Sure . No, nope.

[Clinician asked: "No, OK great. And what about any medications that you take on a regular basis, apart from the, um, combined pill?"] Apart from the pill, um, I take, um, something for the IBS as well. Um it begins with an M, it's pretty hard to pronounce like  something , maybe.
[Clinician asked: "<INAUDIBLE_SPEECH/> was it, yeah OK."] Yeah, yeah that's it. Yeah, yeah that's right.
[Clinician asked: "OK, and is that for the cramps, the crampy pain?"] Yeah, yeah.
[Clinician asked: "Yeah OK, and do you have any allergy to any medications?"] Um, yeah I think there was an antibiotic, um, called  Clindamycin mycin .
[Clinician asked: "OK, OK, OK."] Then I took like  something and yeah that, that didn't, go too well, with me.
[Clinician asked: "Ritib, OK sure, I'll make a note of that. Um, in terms of family history, any history of problems with the bladder or the kidneys?"] No, no.

[Clinician asked: "No, or  generally anything else, good. And socially, tell me a bit more about your home situation, who's at home with you?"] Um, so I am living in halls at the moment, uh at uni um, so uh, uh, I'm just living with a flatmate I suppose, um.

[Clinician asked: "<INAUDIBLE_SPEECH/>"] But my family, uh, live kind of outside of London not too far away. Um, so yeah the my family are fine and I see them now and then. Uh, yeah things I think, I think they're OK generally.
[Clinician asked: "Very good. Great, and what are you studying at university?"] I'm studying, uh, English.

[Clinician asked: "English, OK good, and how are you enjoying your course?"] Yeah, yeah it's it's going well thanks. Just kind of started a few months ago, a few months ago and yeah I, I really enjoy it. Yeah, yeah it's really fun.

[Clinician asked: "Good. Very good, very good, good. Um, in terms of smoking and alcohol, do you, do you participate in either?"] Uh, as I admitted before I tried weed for the first time, uh, but, uh, I don't smoke. Uh ,I drink occasionally, um, kind of socially, um, with friends .
[Clinician asked: "<INAUDIBLE_SPEECH/> O K OK, what, what's made you have your first joint last week? Was"] Um, I  it was just a party,.Um, there were other people doing it, um.
[Clinician asked: "OK."] I kind of was a little bit tipsy so, probably wasn't thinking quite straight. Um, but yeah I, I completely agree it's not something I wanna get into on a regular basis.

[Clinician asked: "<INAUDIBLE_SPEECH/> Sure, uh, and obviously as your doctor I'm here to look after you so yeah I I would strongly recommend you, you know, to avoid if you can. Great, um, fine so, at this stage I wouldn't normally examine you, but I think based on your story I think, um, you know, uh, I'm reassured."] Sure.
[Clinician asked: "But the fact that I, I I think it's, um, probably just a simple what we'd say a UTI or a cystitis, bladder infection."] OK.

[Clinician asked: "Uh, you know you haven't got any other symptoms that, suggest to me that there's anything to worry about, so things like fevers and back pain. Um, would be more worrying for me to hear. Um, so it's something we can treat quite readily with antibiotics. Um, so we've got a choice of two antibiotics so"] <INAUDIBLE_SPEECH/> <INAUDIBLE_SPEECH/> Mm-hmm. Mm-hmm.

[Clinician asked: "And normally they're usually quite effective, um, for your symptoms. It's probably worth giving us a urine sample today as well if you can, so we can send it off to the lab."] <INAUDIBLE_SPEECH/>
[Clinician asked: "And see exactly what's going on there. Um, start the antibiotics, make sure you're well hydrated, drink plenty of fluids."] OK. <INAUDIBLE_SPEECH/>
[Clinician asked: "Um, if you are having some pain, simple Paracetamol, um, would probably be advisable."] Yeah.
[Clinician asked: "Um, we'll see what the urine sample shows. Um, and if it is indeed positive, we'll know which antibiotics are the right ones."] OK.

[Clinician asked: "Um, but if things still are still not getting any better next week come back and see me, and we can have another look again."] OK, OK uh great. Um, with, with the urine sample, um, uh, how do I go about doing that, do you know? Is it through the app?

[Clinician asked: "So if  I'll give yeah no it's a well uh so yeah good question, so I will give you  I will organise uh for you to come in, and drop off a sample at one of our practices, um which we can test there and then, at the urine  and send it off to the lab uh we can back in touch with you with the results."] OK. [Clinician asked: "OK, do bear in mind those symptoms I mentioned about the fever and the back pain and vomiting. If you have any of those give me a call, OK?"] Either back pain, vomiting, great OK. Yeah, I'll watch out for those.

gold_note:
1/52 hx of dysuria, frequency and suprapubic pain. Noticed urine today was pink, nil frank haematuria. 
Suprapubic pain on PU - aching pain, nil radiating 
Nil nausea/vomiting 
Nil fevers 
Nil new bowel sx 
Currently sexually active - long-term partner. Nil PV discharge. LMP 2/52 
E+D well 

PMH: IBS
DH: COCP, mebeverine 
Allergic to clindamycin 
FH: nil
SH: lives in student halls, nil smoking, social EtOH

Imp: UTI

Plan:
1.	Urine dip and MSU
2.	Start abx - nitrofurantoin abx for 3/7 
3.	Push fluids 
4.	Simple analgesia
5.	Review in 1/52 INB, or earlier if worsening sx (fever, back pain, vomiting)

gold_sections.HIGHLIGHTS:
dysuria
frequency
suprapubic pain
IBS
UTI

naive.response_text:
Dear Amanda,

Thank you for sharing your concerns with me. It sounds like you're experiencing symptoms that are quite uncomfortable, such as pain when urinating and the need to go to the bathroom frequently. I understand how this can disrupt your daily life, especially with university commitments.

Based on what you've described, it seems like you might have a urinary tract infection (UTI) or cystitis, which are common and treatable. To confirm this, we'll collect a urine sample, which will help guide the appropriate treatment. In the meantime, I recommend staying well hydrated and using Paracetamol if you're experiencing pain. Please let me know if your symptoms worsen or if you develop any new ones like fever or back pain.

Regarding your recent use of marijuana, I encourage you to avoid it in the future for your overall health. It's not likely related to your current symptoms, but it's important to make healthy choices moving forward.

We'll keep in touch regarding the test results and adjust your care as needed. Please don't hesitate to reach out if you have any questions or concerns.

Take care,
[Your Name, Clinician]

strong_prompt.response_text:
Thank you for sharing all of that with me, Amanda. It sounds like you're feeling quite uncomfortable, and I can understand why this is worrying you. Experiencing pain when you pee, going to the toilet so often, and the pink color in your urine must be really frustrating, especially when you're trying to keep up with uni. I want to make sure we get to the bottom of this and help you feel better.

Based on what you've described, the most likely possibility is a urinary tract infection (UTI) or cystitis, which is inflammation of the bladder. These can cause burning pain, frequent urination, and changes in urine color. The pink tint could be from blood in the urine, which is common in such infections [PMID ?]. I think it's important to confirm this with a urine test, so I'll arrange for you to drop off a sample at one of our practices. This will help us determine the best treatment for you.

In the meantime, I recommend starting on antibiotics to treat the infection, staying well hydrated by drinking plenty of water, and using over-the-counter pain relievers like Paracetamol if you're experiencing discomfort. If your symptoms don't improve within a week or if you develop fever, back pain, or vomiting, please come back or contact me immediately.

We'll follow up in a week to see how you're doing. Could you summarize the plan in your own words so I can make sure everything is clear? Also, I want to encourage you to avoid smoking weed, as it's not ideal for your health. Let me know if you have any questions or concerns in the meantime. Take care, and I'll see you soon.

framework_only.response_text:
I understand you're experiencing pain and discomfort when urinating, along with frequent bathroom visits, which is affecting your ability to attend university. You also mentioned noticing pink-tinged urine and some lower abdominal pain.

We'll start with antibiotics to treat a potential urinary tract infection and advise you to drink plenty of fluids. If your symptoms don't improve within a week, come back for a follow-up visit. We'll also send a urine sample for testing to confirm the diagnosis.

Can you tell me in your own words what we're going to do next and why?

Let's plan to see each other again in a week. In the meantime, if you experience fevers, back pain, or worsening symptoms, please call us right away.

full.response_text:
Thank you for sharing your concerns about your symptoms. I understand you're experiencing painful urination, lower abdominal pain, and suspect IBS. Let's address each of these together.

Painful urination can be caused by a urinary tract infection (UTI). Given the recent onset and associated symptoms like pink-tinged urine and lower abdominal pain, we should consider testing for a UTI.

This pain, especially when going to the toilet, could be related to a UTI or other urinary issues. We'll check for signs of infection to get a clearer picture.

IBS can cause frequent bowel movements and abdominal discomfort. However, we'll first rule out other possible causes for your symptoms to ensure an accurate diagnosis.

We'll start with a urinalysis and urine culture to check for a UTI.

If the results are positive, we'll discuss treatment options like antibiotics.

If UTI is ruled out, we'll explore further causes for your symptoms.

Can you summarize what we're going to do next and why?

Let's plan to follow up in a week to review the test results. If symptoms worsen or you experience new issues, please reach out sooner.

structured_response.acknowledgment: Thank you for sharing your concerns about your symptoms. I understand you're experiencing painful urination, lower abdominal pain, and suspect IBS. Let's address each of these together.

This pain, especially when going to the toilet, could be related to a UTI or other urinary issues. We'll check for signs of infection to get a clearer picture.
IBS can cause frequent bowel movements and abdominal discomfort. However, we'll first rule out other possible causes for your symptoms to ensure an accurate diagnosis.

next_steps: We'll start with a urinalysis and urine culture to check for a UTI.
If the results are positive, we'll discuss treatment options like antibiotics.
If UTI is ruled out, we'll explore further causes for your symptoms.
